rendered:
Hey Karan,

Good to have you on board. I know the desk job grind — 10 hours sitting is no joke, and hitting 3000 steps daily tells me we need to get you moving more, both inside and outside the gym. Your goal is clear: drop to 62 kg in 12 weeks. That's aggressive but doable if we stay consistent. You've got 4 training days a week and a full gym setup, so we're going to build a solid foundation with compounds (squats and rows are in there, as you wanted) and layer in some metabolic work to get that fat loss rolling. Since you're a beginner, we'll keep the volume manageable and focus on learning the movements properly while still creating enough stimulus to see change.

We're running an Upper-Lower split across your 4 days — two lower body sessions and two upper body sessions each week. This gives us frequency without overdoing it, and it fits your evening training window perfectly. I've kept the sessions around 60 minutes, including warm-up and core work.

**Warm-Up Routine (Do this before every training session, around 10 minutes):**
Start with 5 minutes of brisk walking on the treadmill or light cycling to get your heart rate up and blood flowing. Then move into some joint mobility: 10-15 arm circles forward and backward, 10-15 leg swings per leg (front-to-back and side-to-side), 10 bodyweight squats focusing on depth and control, 10 hip openers (step one leg forward into a lunge, drop the back knee, and gently rock forward), and finish with 10 cat-cow stretches to loosen up your spine after all that sitting. Before you get into your main lifts, run through a couple of lighter warm-up sets — maybe the empty bar or very light dumbbells — just to groove the pattern and prep your nervous system.

**Day 1 — Lower Body Power**

Today's about building strength in your legs and posterior chain. Keep your rest periods around 2-3 minutes between the heavy sets on squats and leg press, and closer to 90 seconds on everything else.

- Barbell Back Squat: 4 sets x 5 reps
  (Focus on sitting back into your hips, chest up, and driving through your heels. Don't rush these — quality over ego.)

- Leg Press: 3 sets x 6 reps
  (Feet shoulder-width, push through your whole foot, and control the descent. No bouncing at the bottom.)

- Romanian Deadlift (Barbell): 3 sets x 8 reps
  (Hinge at the hips, slight knee bend, bar stays close to your shins. Feel the stretch in your hamstrings, not your lower back.)

- Leg Curl (Machine): 3 sets x 12 reps
  (Squeeze at the top, slow and controlled on the way down. Don't let momentum take over.)

- Standing Calf Raise (Machine): 3 sets x 15 reps
  (Full range — stretch at the bottom, squeeze at the top. Calves respond to volume, so don't skip these.)

**Core Work:**
- Plank: 3 sets x 30-45 seconds
- Lying Leg Raises: 3 sets x 12 reps
- Russian Twists (Bodyweight or light dumbbell): 3 sets x 20 reps total

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 8000 steps today. You trained hard, but let's keep moving outside the gym too — take a walk after dinner if you can, even 15-20 minutes helps.

---

**Day 2 — Upper Body Push-Pull**

We're hitting both pushing and pulling movements today to balance out your upper body. You mentioned rows are a favorite, so we've got a couple of variations in here. Rest 2 minutes between the big compounds, 90 seconds on accessories.

- Barbell Bench Press: 4 sets x 5 reps
  (Plant your feet, squeeze your shoulder blades together, and press the bar in a slight arc back toward your face. Don't flare your elbows out too wide.)

- Barbell Bent-Over Row: 4 sets x 6 reps
  (Hinge at the hips, keep your back flat, and pull the bar into your lower chest. Think about driving your elbows back, not just yanking with your arms.)

- Dumbbell Shoulder Press (Seated): 3 sets x 8 reps
  (Press straight up, don't arch your lower back. Squeeze your glutes and core to stay stable.)

- Cable Lat Pulldown (Wide Grip): 3 sets x 10 reps
  (Pull down to your upper chest, lean back slightly, and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom.)

- Dumbbell Chest Fly (Flat Bench): 3 sets x 12 reps
  (Control the stretch at the bottom, don't go too deep if your shoulders feel cranky. Squeeze your chest at the top.)

- Cable Face Pull: 3 sets x 15 reps
  (Pull the rope toward your face, elbows high, and squeeze your rear delts. Great for posture after all that desk sitting.)

**Core Work:**
- Bicycle Crunches: 3 sets x 20 reps total
- Dead Bug: 3 sets x 10 reps per side
- Side Plank: 3 sets x 20-30 seconds per side

**Daily Steps:** Target 8000 steps. You're training again, so keep the movement going throughout the day — set a timer to stand and walk every hour at work if you can.

---

**Day 3 — Active Recovery**

No gym today, but we're not sitting still. This is about moving your body gently, improving mobility, and flushing out any soreness from the first two days. Do 20-30 minutes of light activity — a brisk walk outside, some easy cycling, or even yoga if that's your thing. Follow it up with 15 minutes of stretching and mobility work: hip flexor stretches, hamstring stretches, thoracic spine rotations, and some foam rolling if your gym has rollers. Think of this as maintenance, not punishment.

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 10,000 steps today. No heavy lifting, so let's prioritize general movement. Walk to grab lunch, take the stairs, go for an evening stroll — whatever fits your day.

---

**Day 4 — Lower Body Hypertrophy**

We're back in the gym, but today's lower body work is higher reps and more volume compared to Day 1. The goal is to build muscle and burn energy. Rest periods are shorter here — 90 seconds to 2 minutes max.

- Goblet Squat (Dumbbell): 3 sets x 10 reps
  (Hold a dumbbell at your chest, squat deep, and keep your torso upright. Great for learning squat mechanics and hitting your quads.)

- Bulgarian Split Squat (Dumbbell): 3 sets x 10 reps per leg
  (Rear foot elevated on a bench, front foot far enough forward that your knee doesn't shoot past your toes. These are humbling but gold for your glutes and quads.)

- Leg Press: 3 sets x 15 reps
  (Higher reps today, so drop the weight a bit. Focus on control and really squeezing your quads at the top.)

- Leg Curl (Machine): 3 sets x 15 reps
  (Same as Day 1, but more reps. Feel the burn in your hamstrings.)

- Walking Lunges (Dumbbell): 3 sets x 12 reps per leg
  (Step forward, drop your back knee toward the ground, and drive through your front heel to stand. Keep your torso upright and core tight.)

- Seated Calf Raise (Machine): 3 sets x 20 reps
  (Targets the soleus muscle under your gastrocnemius. Full range, slow tempo.)

**Core Work:**
- Hanging Knee Raises (or Lying Leg Raises if no pull-up bar): 3 sets x 10 reps
- Cable Woodchop: 3 sets x 12 reps per side
- Plank with Shoulder Taps: 3 sets x 20 taps total

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 8000 steps. Training day, so the gym work counts, but still try to move outside of it.

---

**Day 5 — Upper Body Hypertrophy**

Last training day of the week, and we're finishing strong with higher-rep upper body work. This is where we build muscle and torch some calories. Rest 90 seconds between sets, and on the last set of isolation moves, if you hit strict failure and have a bit left in the tank, you can squeeze out 1-2 controlled cheat reps — but only on isolation stuff like flys or curls, never on the big barbell lifts.

- Dumbbell Bench Press (Flat): 3 sets x 8 reps
  (Press the dumbbells up and slightly inward, squeezing your chest at the top. Control the descent.)

- Cable Seated Row (Close Grip): 3 sets x 10 reps
  (Pull the handle into your lower ribs, squeeze your shoulder blades, and keep your torso stable. Don't rock back and forth.)

- Dumbbell Incline Press: 3 sets x 10 reps
  (Bench set to 30-45 degrees. Targets your upper chest. Press straight up, not forward.)

- Dumbbell Single-Arm Row: 3 sets x 12 reps per arm
  (One knee and hand on a bench, row the dumbbell up toward your hip. Focus on pulling with your back, not your bicep.)

- Cable Lateral Raise: 3 sets x 15 reps
  (Stand sideways to the cable, raise your arm out to the side, and control the descent. Great for your side delts.)

- Dumbbell Bicep Curl: 3 sets x 12 reps
  (No swinging. Curl up, squeeze at the top, control the descent. If you get 12 strict reps and have a bit left, you can do 1-2 cheat reps on the last set only.)

- Cable Tricep Pushdown (Rope): 3 sets x 15 reps
  (Elbows pinned to your sides, push down and squeeze your triceps at the bottom. Control the return.)

**Core Work:**
- Cable Crunch: 3 sets x 15 reps
- Mountain Climbers: 3 sets x 20 reps total
- Plank (with weight on back if too easy): 3 sets x 30-45 seconds

**Daily Steps:** Target 8000 steps. Last training day, so finish strong and keep moving throughout the day.

---

**Day 6 — Rest Day**

Full rest today. No gym, no structured activity. Your body needs this to recover and adapt. Focus on sleep, hydration, and eating well. If you feel like doing something, a gentle 15-minute walk is fine, but don't push it.

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 6000 steps. It's a rest day, so we're scaling back, but don't be completely sedentary. A short walk after meals is enough.

---

**Day 7 — Rest Day**

Another full rest day to close out the week. Use this time to prep for the week ahead — meal prep if you can, sort out your gym bag, and get to bed early. Your body is rebuilding right now, so let it do its thing.

**Daily Steps:** Aim for 6000 steps. Same as yesterday — light movement is fine, but prioritize recovery.

---

**Post-Workout Stretching Routine (Do this after every training session, around 10 minutes):**

After you finish your workout and core work, spend 10 minutes stretching the major muscle groups you just trained. Hold each stretch for 30-60 seconds per side, breathe deeply, and don't bounce. Here's a simple flow: standing quad stretch (pull your heel to your glutes), seated hamstring stretch (reach for your toes with legs extended), hip flexor stretch (lunge position, push your hips forward), chest stretch (hands behind your back or against a wall, open up your chest), lat stretch (reach one arm overhead and lean to the opposite side), and shoulder stretch (pull one arm across your body). If you trained lower body, spend extra time on your hips and hamstrings. If you trained upper body, focus on your chest, shoulders, and lats. This will help with recovery and keep you mobile, especially important given how much you're sitting during the day.

---

**Training Intensity Guidance:**

Let's talk about how hard to push. On your big compound lifts — squats, bench press, rows, deadlifts, leg press — you should leave 1-2 reps in the tank. That means if you could do 7 reps, stop at 5. This keeps the quality high and reduces injury risk while you're still learning the movements. On isolation and accessory work — leg curls, flys, curls, lateral raises — you can push closer to failure. If you hit 12 reps and feel like you have 1-2 left, that's perfect. On the very last set of isolation exercises only, if you hit strict failure and still have a bit of energy, you can do 1-2 controlled cheat reps to squeeze out a bit more stimulus — but this is never allowed on squats, deadlifts, RDLs, lunges, or any barbell compound movement. Those always stay strict and controlled. No exceptions.

---

**Sleep and Recovery:**

You're getting 7-8 hours now, which is solid, but let's tighten it up a bit. Aim for 7.5-9 hours every night, ideally going to bed by 11 pm and waking up around 7-7:30 am to match your 9:30 am breakfast. Since you're training in the evening and eating dinner late (10:30 pm), try to finish your last meal by 10 pm if possible, and keep it lighter — your current late biryani deliveries are likely sitting heavy and messing with your sleep quality. Here are a few practical tips suited to your routine: cut off chai and coffee by 4 pm (I know it's tough, but that 6:30 pm tea with chana might be keeping you wired), dim the lights and reduce screen time at least 30 minutes before bed (no scrolling through Instagram or watching YouTube in bed), keep your room cool and dark (use a fan or AC if it's hot, blackout curtains help), try to eat your last meal at least 90 minutes before bed so digestion doesn't interfere with sleep, if you're stressed from work, spend 5-10 minutes doing deep breathing or light stretching before bed to wind down, and keep your wake-up time consistent even on weekends — your body loves routine. Better sleep means better recovery, better fat loss, and better performance in the gym, so don't skip this part.

---

Alright, Karan, that's your week. Four solid training days, two full rest days, and one active recovery day. Your step targets are built in, your core work is varied, and we've got a good mix of strength and hypertrophy work to get you moving toward that 62 kg goal. The key here is consistency — show up, do the work, and trust the process. You've got this. Let me know how the first week goes, and we'll adjust as needed.
